perturbation.original_text: The parties warrant that they have the right to make the disclosures under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties believe that they have the right to make the disclosures under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'warrant' with 'believe' significantly weakens the assurance. A warranty is a guarantee; a belief is simply an opinion. This creates ambiguity about the actual legal basis for disclosing the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) of British Columbia
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 11(1) of the British Columbia PIPA states that an organization must ensure that it has the authority to collect, use or disclose personal information. The modified clause introduces doubt as to whether the parties truly *have* the right, contradicting the requirement to ensure they have this right. 'Believe' suggests uncertainty where certainty is required by the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Legislation - Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner for BC English français Deutsch italiano español 中文(简体) русский 한국어 Contact us Follow @BCInfoPrivacy LinkedIn RSS Proactive Disclosure Advanced Search Home About Legislation Legislation The Information and Privacy Commissioner enforces two pieces of legislation: the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (FIPPA) and the Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA). The Privacy Act covers disputes between private citizens and is outside of the Commissioner's jurisdiction. Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act (Public Sector) The Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act sets out the access and privacy rights of individuals as they relate to the public sector. FIPPA establishes an individual's right to access records, including access to a person's own "personal information" as well as records in the custody or control of a "public body" -- see Schedule 2 and Schedule 3 for a list of public bodies that are covered by FIPPA. There are certain exceptions to accessing records - for example, a public body cannot disclose information that would be harmful to law enforcement, personal privacy or public safety. Policy advice and legal advice are also excluded. These exceptions are explained in sections 12 to 22 . In addition to establishing an individual's right to access records, FIPPA also sets out the terms under which a public body can collect, use and disclose the " personal information " of individuals. Public bodies are accountable for their information practices; FIPPA requires that they take reasonable steps to protect the privacy of personal information they hold. Public bodies are required to have privacy management programs in place and to report privacy breaches that could reasonably be expected to result in harm to the affected individuals and to the OIPC. Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Regulation Committees of the Executive Council Regulation Sectional Index (access to OIPC Orders by section of FIPPA) Delegation of Powers, Duties and Functions Personal Information Protection Act (Private Sector) British Columbia's Personal Information Protection Act came into effect in January 2004. The legislation applies to any private sector organization (such as a business or corporation, union, political party, and not-for-profit) that collects, uses, and discloses the personal information of individuals in BC. PIPA also applies to any organization located within BC that collects, uses, or discloses personal information of any individual inside or outside of BC. Under PIPA, individuals have the right to access their own personal information. The law also states the rules by which organizations can collect, use and disclose personal information from customers, clients and/or employees. PIPA requires organizations to protect and secure personal information against unauthorized use or disclosure. Personal Information Protection Act Personal Information Protection Act Regulations Sectional Index (access to OIPC Orders by section of PIPA) Delegation of Powers, Duties and Functions E-Health (Personal Health Information Access and Protection of Privacy) Act E-Health (Personal Health Information Access and Protection of Privacy) Act E-Health Act Regulation Disclosure Directive Regulation Delegation of Powers, Duties and Functions Legislative Reviews Every six years, FIPPA and PIPA are reviewed by a Special Committee of the Legislature . The Committee asks the public and interest groups for written and oral submissions that describe how the law is working and whether changes are needed. At the end of the process, the Committee issues a public report that sets out recommendations going forward. For more information, visit the Legislative Assembly website. perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'warrant' which implies a guarantee, while the changed text uses 'believe' which suggests an opinion and introduces uncertainty. The Law Explanation argues that this weakens the assurance regarding the right to disclose personal information. Section 11(1) of the British Columbia PIPA requires organizations to 'ensure that it has the authority to collect, use or disclose personal information.' The changed text contradicts this requirement because 'believe' suggests uncertainty where certainty is required by law. The scraped law snippet regarding the Personal Information Protection Act (Private Sector) states, 'PIPA requires organizations to protect and secure personal information against unauthorized use or disclosure.' By weakening the assurance that parties have the right to make disclosures, the change increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure. The 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition states that legal contradictions occur when a legal statement is vague, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law, which applies here.
